summary: This house was built by Doctor John Berry in 1809. He was a very prominent person in our community, though he never married or had children. The house has a rich history, and owning it has been a significant part of my life since I bought it in 2005.

I moved to Bath County in 2006 after buying this farm in 2005. Living here has been part of fulfilling my dream, especially with the antiques shows I hold on the farm. It’s a peaceful place with lots of land, perfect for my antiques business and lifestyle.

I was born and raised in Mount Sterling, Kentucky, specifically at the Levee, and I was born in Mary Chiles Hospital there. Mount Sterling holds a special place in my heart, especially with events like Court Day where I’ve been selling antiques for about six years. It’s also where I rent spaces for my antiques at Court Day, right behind the Athletic Club.

I was born in Mary Chiles Hospital in Mount Sterling, Kentucky. It’s part of my early life story and roots in the community. Being born there connects me to Mount Sterling and the surrounding area where I grew up.

A good friend and neighbor from Texas, Diane Williams, introduced me to antiques and inspired my love for them. She showed me the ropes, took me to antique shops, and shared Country Living magazines that educated me. Even though she moved back to Texas, we still keep in touch.

Diane Williams was a sweet friend and neighbor from Texas who really inspired my antiques career. She took me to antique shops and shared her collection of Country Living magazines, which helped educate me. Even after she moved back to Texas, we’ve stayed in contact, and I credit her with sparking my love for antiques.

In 2001, I bought a building in Frenchburg, Kentucky, to start my antiques business called Raggedy Treasures. At that time, I was living in Jeffersonville, Kentucky, and ran the shop until my child started kindergarten. Frenchburg was the first place I really tested my antiques business.

When I started my antiques business, I was living in Jeffersonville, Kentucky. From there, I bought a building in Frenchburg to run my shop. Jeffersonville was home before moving to the farm in Bath County.

Raggedy Treasures was the name of my first antiques shop, which I opened around 2001 in Frenchburg, Kentucky. The name came from my friend Diane Williams’ love of Raggedy Ann dolls. I ran Raggedy Treasures until my child started kindergarten, then sold it and moved my business to the farm.

I do one of my biggest antiques shows at the Kentucky Horse Park every spring. It’s a two-space show where I set up themed items like garden pots and iron furniture. The Horse Park show is one of the highlights of my antiques selling year.

I participate in Vintage Market Days in Xenia, Ohio, every April. It’s a one-space antiques show where I bring items that fit the seasonal theme. Xenia is one of several places outside Kentucky where I sell antiques.

Ball-Tiques is the name of my current antiques business, named after Ball-Tara Farm where I live. It’s a play on the farm’s name and reflects my passion for antiques. I do multiple shows a year under the Ball-Tiques name, including ones on the farm and at the Kentucky Horse Park.

Ball-Tara Farm is the 120-acre property I bought in 2005 in Bath County. The name comes from Scarlett’s dream in Gone With the Wind, which inspired me. It’s where I live, run my antiques business Ball-Tiques, and hold several antiques shows each year.
cues: named after Gone With the Wind

summary: Gone With the Wind inspired the name Ball-Tara Farm, which is my home and antiques business location. Scarlett’s dream in the story resonated with me, and I wanted to create my own dream here. It’s a meaningful connection that ties my farm to that classic work.

Court Day in Mount Sterling has been a big part of my life since childhood. I grew up going every year and now sell antiques there, renting three parking spaces behind the Athletic Club. It feels like a homecoming each time, connecting my past and present in the community.

I was born in Montgomery County, Kentucky, and spent my early years there before moving to Bath County. Growing up in Montgomery County, I attended events like Court Day and have many fond memories from that time. It’s where my roots and family history lie.
